item.title: Nordic leaders meet in Oslo with Carney as Arctic tensions sharpen over Greenland, Russia
item.description: Leaders from the five Nordic countries and Canada meet in Oslo for a mini-summit on Arctic security amid rising geopolitical tensions. Discussions follow a massive NATO exercise in northern Norway and focus on Russian military risks, the fallout from U.S. threats to seize Greenland and energy security.

item.title: Inside Toronto’s massive ping pong boom — and the high-stakes battle for table space
Thousands march at pro-Palestinian rally in Toronto after judge denies Ford government’s injunction to stop it
item.description: Al-Quds Day is an annual event to show support for Palestine. Critics have called it antisemitic.

item.title: Prime Minister’s office declines to weigh in on Order of Canada petition for Don Cherry
item.description: Conservative MP gathering signatures for online petition to award Canada’s highest honorary distinction to hockey commentator

item.title: Woman seriously injured in family stabbing at Scarborough residence: police
Carney defends his economic record after one year in office
item.description: Prime Minister is in Norway as part of ongoing efforts to open new trade and business opportunities

item.title: Toronto custodian faces 85 counts of voyeurism linked to hidden camera at Whitby workplace
Snow, freezing rain, strong winds headed for Toronto on Sunday, special weather statement says
Canada’s Olympic medal letdown masks the real sports crisis in this country. One potential game changer is already on the table
Carney, Norway PM push message of oil-market stability as war in Middle East rattles global supply
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney and Norway’s Jonas Gahr Støre stressed energy stability during talks in Oslo as the Middle East war rattles global oil markets. Canada will release 23.6 million barrels following an International Energy Agency call for coordinated action. Both leaders also urged de-escalation and pledged closer Canada-Norway cooperation on Arctic security.

item.title: Carney government in talks with Saskatchewan to fast-track energy projects
item.description: Mark Carney’s government is in talks with Saskatchewan towards reaching an energy deal that could fast-track federal approvals of that provinc…

item.title: Michael Ford reveals why he may run for mayor of Toronto — and what he’s done since leaving Queen’s Park
Why Canada is helping Canadians flee the Middle East — and what it costs
‘One elbow up, one down’: The Carney government after its first year
Carney says Canada a reliable oil exporter with moves to increase production
Federal government planning to revamp funding for Canadian athletes, says Carney
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney says Ottawa is planning to revamp its funding for Canadian athletes and will examine the issue "very deliberately" over the next six months — news that comes just before a federal commission on the future of Canadian sport releases its final report.

item.title: New café offers taste of Quebec independence
item.description: When you walk into the Club Pays coffee shop in Montreal, the first thing you see are the posters with Quebec sovereignty slogans plastered on every wall.

item.title: Doug Ford to seek an injunction to block Al-Quds Day rally in Toronto
item.description: The premier's move comes in the wake of a similar move by two city councillors.

item.title: He’s useful in a crisis – but is he any good at politics? A look at Mark Carney’s first year
item.description: For the first time, Canada has a Prime Minister figuring out how to be a politician on the job, in front of everyone

item.title: Canadians might be soon waiting longer to have their air travel complaints heard
item.description: The federal government is currently weighing whether to renew funding meant to address a massive backlog of airline passenger complaints — funding that if not extended could result in Canadians having to wait longer to have their cases heard.

item.title: Former NATO chief says he doesn't think allies will be pulled further into Middle East conflict
item.description: The former secretary general of NATO says he doesn't think allies will be pulled further into the conflict in the Middle East, but he's concerned the ongoing war with Iran will benefit Russia's economy and take the world’s attention away from the invasion of Ukraine.

item.title: Parks Canada planning to cull dozens of deer on historic Quebec island
item.description: Parks Canada is proposing to kill dozens of white-tailed deer at a storied historic site near Quebec City, saying the deer population on the island presents a threat to rare plants and the safety of employees flying in. However, an animal-rights advocate is calling for the agency to use non-lethal solutions to deal with the deer.

item.title: Quebec Conservatives sour on Don Cherry nomination as party pushes Order of Canada petition
item.description: Opposition is mounting among Quebec's federal Conservatives to their own party's push to award the Order of Canada to controversial hockey commentator Don Cherry.

item.title: Federal departments, agencies release details on plans to find billions in internal savings
item.description: Reductions include lower than planned spending for a construction project at Transport Canada and scaling back legal support for veterans looking to appeal rulings about their benefits

item.title: Ford government to end funding for remaining supervised consumption sites: advocates
Ottawa to allow rural employers to increase proportion of temporary foreign workers
item.description: Ottawa will allow rural employers to have a greater proportion of low-wage temporary foreign workers on their payroll on a time-limited basis, as part of new measures the federal government announced on Friday.

item.title: ‘We’re ready to defend the Arctic,’ Carney says alongside German, Norwegian leaders
Mark Carney rejects Trump administration move to ease sanctions on Russian oil
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney disagreed Friday with a U.S. move to ease sanctions on Russian oil, joining world leaders who want to keep restrict…

item.title: Mark Carney helping push Liberals ahead of the Conservatives, poll suggests
Premier and cabinet ministers will be exempt from FOI requests under new legislation
item.description: The Ontario government says it will be introducing legislation to keep secret documents from the premier’s office as well as the offices of his cabinet ministers.

item.title: Conservative leader says his plan is ‘the only hope’ for Canada’s auto industry
Politics Insider: Carney rebukes U.S. for easing sanctions on Russian oil sales
item.description: Ontario to introduce legislation exempting the Premier, cabinet ministers from FOI requests

item.title: Ottawa to extend temporary work permits in Quebec for up to 12 months
Doug Ford is changing a law to shield politicians from scrutiny
U.S. begins forced-labour investigation into 60 countries, including Canada
item.description: Offending nations could face tariffs of as much as 25%, analysts say

item.title: Toronto Al-Quds Day rally to go as planned despite Ford’s call for injunction: lawyer
item.description: Ford posted a video on social media saying he had instructed the attorney general to seek an injunction against the demonstration.

item.title: As Trump pauses Russian shadow fleet sanctions, Carney says Canada holding firm
item.description: Three world leaders — two of them members of the G7 — stood united in opposition Friday to the Trump administration's decision to ease sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet of oil tankers.

item.title: Unions, MPs warn funding cuts could affect services for veterans
Questions linger about duties of incoming national security and intelligence adviser
IN PHOTOS | Highlights from NATO's Cold Response exercise
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Store were near Bardufoss, Norway, to watch NATO's Cold Response. The joint military exercises, which Norway hosts every two years, involves 32,000 troops, 100 aircraft and 30 warships from 14 nations.

item.title: Ontario to give retailers the option of opening on Family Day and Victoria Day
Carney says Canada's job creation still way ahead of U.S. despite losing 84,000 jobs last month
item.description: The Canadian economy lost more jobs in February than it has in a single month in the last four years — but Prime Minister Mark Carney says when compared to the United States, we're doing pretty good.

item.title: Canada rejects Iraqi government’s claim that Ottawa will take detainees from ISIL era
Environment committee engaging in ‘junior high behaviour’: former climate advisers
item.description: OTTAWA - Two former advisers on Ottawa's climate policies say recent antics by parliamentarians at the House of Commons environment committee …

item.title: How John Tory and Michael Ford have shaken up Toronto mayor’s race, according to a new poll
Doug Ford says Ontario will expropriate Billy Bishop airport to bring jets in
item.description: The premier said he told Mayor Olivia Chow about his intention to expropriate the island airport during a recent conversation at his home.

item.title: Canada's economy lost 84,000 jobs in February, unemployment rate ticked up to 6.7%
item.description: Canada's economy lost 84,000 jobs in February while the unemployment rate edged up to 6.7 per cent, Statistics Canada said on Friday, a setback for the labour market and one of the worst monthly job losses seen in years outside of the pandemic.

item.title: Conservatives nominate candidate in Scarborough byelection
Submarines off the table as Carney meets with leaders of Norway, Germany
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney touched down in Norway early Friday where he will get important face time with the Nordic country’s leader and the German chancellor before taking in a major NATO wargaming exercise that includes Canadian troops.

item.title: Canada considers joining U.K., Italy and Japan to develop future fighter jets
item.description: The post Canada considers joining U.K., Italy and Japan to develop future fighter jets appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: How rare is it for a prime minister to attract 4 floor-crossers in 4 months?
item.description: Floor-crossing has always been a feature of Canadian politics dating back to the first Parliament — and seeing four opposition MPs jump ship to join the government in a matter of months is rare but not unprecedented.

item.title: Poilievre set to unveil Conservative auto pact back in Canada's car capital Sunday
item.description: Canada's Opposition leader says his new plan would take GST off Canadian-made cars and eliminate tariffs to sell them in the U.S.

item.title: ‘We’re ready’: Liberal candidate says ahead of Terrebonne byelection rematch
item.description: Liberal Tatiana Auguste faces Bloc candidate Nathalie Sinclair-Desgagné in an April 13 byelection triggered by a one-vote result overturned by the Supreme Court.

item.title: Carney to discuss energy security during three-day trip to Norway
item.description: Oil-rich Scandinavian country is not profiting from the Iran war, its Prime Minister says

item.title: Carney’s $35B Arctic military base upgrade plan will please Trump: expert
item.description: Carney also said four northern projects, including the Arctic Economic and Security Corridor, are being referred to the Major Projects Office for expedited review and approval.

item.title: Current and former politicians in Nunavut support MP’s decision to cross floor
item.description: It’s far easier to influence policy from the side of government than in opposition, says Peter Ittinuar, the first Inuk ever elected to Parliament

item.title: Carney announces $35B for defence, infrastructure in Canada's North
item.description: The Liberal government has unveiled a detailed, multibillion-dollar proposal to modernize and expand Canada's military footprint in the country's Far North.

item.title: Canada to spend nearly $35-billion to fortify North, assert sovereignty
item.description: Funds will pay for upgraded airfields, new or repurposed hangars, ammunition and fuel storage, among other improvements

item.title: Carney to meet with King Charles III during trip to United Kingdom
item.description: The meeting, set for the afternoon of March 16, will be the third time Carney and the King have meet since Carney became prime minister a year ago.

item.title: Senate’s amendment for Bill C-4 rejected, government vows to protect Canadians’ privacy
item.description: Red Chamber pushed back on section that exempts political parties from provincial privacy law

item.title: Silence from Ottawa 'shameful' after report of Canadian camp hit by missile: Conservative MP
item.description: Conservative defence critic James Bezan says it's "really shameful" the federal government did not tell the public that the airbase in Kuwait, where the Canadian Armed Forces have a camp, was hit by an Iranian missile attack on March 1.

item.title: Tories slam Liberals for not disclosing strike on Kuwait military base
item.description: Canada has an operational support hub at the Ali Al-salem Air Base, which also hosts the U.S. Air Force and was reportedly hit by an Iranian missile on March 1.

item.title: Russian hackers targeting Canada’s governments, businesses and military, cyber spy agency warns
item.description: Dutch intelligence says Signal, WhatsApp accounts are being targeted because they provide end-to-end encryption

item.title: Liberals renew push for police access to digital communications
item.description: The post Liberals renew push for police access to digital communications appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Canada pledges $37.7-million in Lebanon aid
item.description: UN, Red Cross and other NGOs will facilitate the distribution of the support

item.title: Ottawa says Musqueam deal unrelated to private property rights
item.description: Both the government and First Nation say private land is not on the table, but that is not spelled out in the agreements

item.title: Niagara regional chair’s resignation over Hitler book has Ford government ‘reviewing its options’
Make teaching more attractive for tradespeople, teachers' federation tells Ontario
item.description: Ontario must make entering the teaching profession more attractive to skilled tradespeople if it hopes to address a long-standing shortage of technological education teachers and ultimately get more students into the trades, a teachers organization says.

item.title: Bluebell II, Island Chickadee or Water Strider? Voting underway to name Toronto’s new electric ferries
Simcoe County students express opposition to OSAP cuts by the Ontario government
item.description: High school students in Barrie traded in their books for protest signs on Wednesday to fight the Ontario government’s planned changes to the province’s student financial aid program.

item.title: New lawful access bill would give police, CSIS more powers to track suspects online
item.description: The Liberal government has introduced a new lawful access bill that it says will help police and security services track and identify people who may be using tools like social media or artificial intelligence to commit crimes or threaten national security.

item.title: Police will get new powers for online data in tweaked ‘lawful access’ bill
item.description: Bill C-22 narrows the scope of the original legislation that sought to give police new powers to access internet user data from service providers.

item.title: Ottawa limits scope of lawful access bill after outcry
item.description: Critics of the original legislation decried warrantless searches of services Canadians use

item.title: Here’s why the Weston family’s multimillion-dollar revamp of Queen’s Park North is getting closer scrutiny
Carney set to fly to Yellowknife then Norway with defence-focused agenda
item.description: Carney will be the first Canadian prime minister to make an official visit to Norway since 1980. Heads of government from Iceland, Denmark, Sweden and Finland will also be present.

item.title: ‘Those days are done:’ Doug Ford says elementary teachers will no longer have to pay out of pocket for supplies
item.description: Ontario elementary school teachers will no longer have to pay out of pocket for classroom supplies and will soon have access to an annual $750 spending account to cover the cost, Premier Doug Ford says.

item.title: Carney heads to Norway for defence-focused trip with Nordic countries
item.description: Prime Minister is planning to observe NATO Cold Response exercises, designed to enhance the alliance’s Arctic readiness

item.title: Alberta separation talk creating business uncertainty, province’s chambers of commerce say
item.description: More than half of businesses and investors surveyed say separation issue is affecting the provincial economy

item.title: Ontario is bracing for ‘tougher times,’ finance minister warns
